text: Last class you handled hard questions and stayed honest under pressure.
Today you sit at the table: How do you open a deal and propose terms politely?
We would like to propose a three-year deal.
If you accept a longer contract, we would lower the price.
Would you be open to a compromise?
Thank you for coming. We would like to propose a partnership for three seasons.
Thank you. Our offer is two seasons at the same yearly value you proposed.
If you accept three seasons, we could offer more visibility on the shirts. That is our counteroffer.
We also want exclusive rights in every category. I am afraid we may be at a deadlock there.
Let us look for common ground. Would you be open to a compromise: exclusive rights in two categories?
That works. We can meet halfway. I think we have a deal.
Let us look for common ground and meet halfway.

Hello Doctor Nilo, this is Sofia again from the FIFA Leadership in Football program office. Today we move to one of the most useful skills in your work: opening a negotiation. Soon you will sit at the table to negotiate a sponsorship contract for Corinthians, so here is some practical advice. First, always open warmly and state what you want clearly, but politely. Instead of a hard demand, propose. You can say, we would like to propose a three-year deal, or, we were hoping to agree on better terms. Second, use soft language with would and could. We could offer more visibility, or, if you accept a longer contract, we would lower the yearly price. This sounds professional and keeps the door open. Third, listen for the other side's offer and their counteroffer, and look for common ground. A good negotiator never wins everything; both sides meet halfway and reach a fair compromise. Fourth, if the talk reaches a deadlock and nobody moves, do not lose your temper. Stay calm, suggest a short break, or say, let me get back to you with a new proposal. And finally, know your limit before you start, so you understand when a deal is good and when it is better to walk away. Remember, Doctor, the goal is not to defeat the other side. The goal is a deal that works for both clubs. Good luck at the table.

Let me tell you how my first sponsorship negotiation in English went. I represented Corinthians, and across the table was the marketing director of a large sponsor. I opened politely. I said, thank you for coming, we would like to propose a partnership for three seasons. He smiled and made his offer, but the yearly value was lower than we expected. So I did not say no directly. Instead, I used soft language. I said, we could accept that value if you increase the visibility on our shirts and at the stadium. That was my counteroffer. He thought about it and we started to look for common ground. For a while we were close to a deadlock, because he wanted exclusive rights and we wanted flexibility. I stayed calm and suggested a compromise: we would meet halfway, giving him exclusive rights in two categories instead of all of them. He liked that. Before the meeting, I had decided my limit, so I knew exactly how far I could go and when I would have to walk away. In the end, we did not walk away. We shook hands on a fair deal that worked for both sides. To open and to soften every proposal, I used would and could all the time, and that kept the whole negotiation friendly and professional. It was the first time I felt confident closing a deal in English.
We would like to propose a three-year deal.
If you accept a longer contract, we would lower the price.
We could offer more visibility on the shirts.
Let us look for common ground.
Could we meet halfway and find a fair compromise?
Would you be open to a compromise?

Here is how to open a negotiation well. First, greet the other side and open warmly. Then, propose your terms politely with would like to. After that, listen to their offer and make a counteroffer. Next, look for common ground and suggest a compromise. Finally, close the deal or, if it is not good enough, walk away.
Let us look for common ground and meet halfway.
